title: Kennedy Elected President
summary: John F. Kennedy narrowly defeats Richard Nixon to become the 35th President of the United States. Kennedy wins with 49.7% of the popular vote, the closest election in decades. He is the first Catholic president.
impact: Signals generational change in American politics and energizes liberal hopes for civil rights progress.

title: Bay of Pigs Invasion
summary: CIA-trained Cuban exiles attempt to overthrow Fidel Castro by landing at the Bay of Pigs in Cuba. The invasion fails within three days. Kennedy takes public responsibility for the disaster.
impact: Humiliates the new administration and escalates Cold War tensions with the Soviet Union.

title: Cuban Missile Crisis
summary: U.S. reconnaissance photographs reveal Soviet nuclear missiles being installed in Cuba, 90 miles from Florida. Kennedy orders a naval blockade. For thirteen days the world stands at the brink of nuclear war before Khrushchev agrees to remove the missiles.
impact: The closest the Cold War comes to nuclear conflict. Ordinary Americans confront the real possibility of annihilation.

title: March on Washington
summary: Over 250,000 people march on Washington D.C. for jobs and freedom. Martin Luther King Jr. delivers his 'I Have a Dream' speech from the steps of the Lincoln Memorial. It is the largest demonstration in American history to date.
impact: Galvanizes national support for civil rights legislation and becomes the defining moment of the movement.

title: President Kennedy Assassinated
summary: President John F. Kennedy is shot and killed while riding in a motorcade through Dealey Plaza in Dallas, Texas. Lee Harvey Oswald is arrested. Vice President Lyndon Johnson is sworn in aboard Air Force One.
impact: Traumatizes the nation. Johnson pledges to continue Kennedy's legislative agenda, including civil rights.

title: Civil Rights Act Signed
summary: President Johnson signs the Civil Rights Act of 1964, outlawing discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin. The act bans segregation in public places and employment discrimination. It passes after a 75-day Senate filibuster.
impact: The most significant civil rights legislation since Reconstruction. Transforms the legal landscape of American race relations.

title: Bloody Sunday in Selma
summary: Six hundred civil rights marchers attempting to walk from Selma to Montgomery, Alabama are attacked by state troopers with tear gas and clubs on the Edmund Pettus Bridge. Television cameras broadcast the violence nationally.
impact: The brutality shocks the nation and directly leads to the Voting Rights Act of 1965.

title: Detroit Riots Begin
summary: A police raid on an unlicensed bar on 12th Street triggers five days of civil unrest in Detroit. The National Guard and U.S. Army paratroopers are deployed. 43 people are killed, over 1,000 injured, and more than 2,000 buildings are destroyed.
impact: Devastates Detroit's inner city and accelerates white flight to the suburbs. Exposes the limits of civil rights progress in northern cities.

title: Martin Luther King Jr. Assassinated
summary: Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. is shot and killed on the balcony of the Lorraine Motel in Memphis, Tennessee, where he had gone to support striking sanitation workers. Riots erupt in over 100 American cities.
impact: Removes the most prominent voice for nonviolent change and deepens the crisis in American race relations.

title: Moon Landing
summary: Apollo 11 astronauts Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin become the first humans to walk on the moon. Armstrong's first step is broadcast live to an estimated 600 million television viewers worldwide. The mission fulfills Kennedy's 1961 pledge.
impact: A moment of national unity and technological triumph at the end of a turbulent decade.
